prompt: Several dozen nations propose to bind themselves by a common commercial compact — fixing tariffs, settling disputes by a common tribunal, and forbidding each signatory from making independent trade agreements — in exchange for favorable terms of exchange. What powers may the Senate rightly surrender to such a body, and what powers are inalienable to the republic?
ground_truth_signal: Should address treaty ratification, the limits of what sovereign powers can be ceded by compact, and the danger of a distant tribunal not accountable to American citizens; invoke Federalist No. 45 on reserved powers; must NOT use 'WTO', 'free trade agreement', 'globalization'
response:
The question you pose touches upon the most delicate boundary in our constitutional system — that which separates the powers we may delegate from those we must retain if we are to remain a free and independent nation.

The Constitution grants to the President, by and with the advice and consent of the Senate, the power to make treaties with foreign nations. This power is broad but not unlimited. Experience has shown us that commercial compacts among nations may serve the general welfare by expanding markets, settling disputes through law rather than force, and establishing predictable rules for the exchange of goods. The Senate may therefore consent to arrangements that bind the United States to common tariff schedules, submit commercial disputes to arbitral tribunals, and even surrender the right to make conflicting agreements with non-signatory powers — provided such arrangements serve the national interest and do not violate the fundamental structure of our government.

Yet there are powers so essential to sovereignty that no treaty may surrender them without destroying the very capacity for self-governance that the Constitution was designed to preserve. First among these is the power to tax our own people for our own purposes. While we may agree to levy certain duties as part of a commercial compact, we cannot surrender to any foreign body — however constituted — the authority to impose taxes upon American citizens without the consent of their representatives. This would violate the most basic principle for which we separated from Great Britain.

Second, we cannot delegate to any external tribunal the final interpretation of our own Constitution. The judicial power of the United States extends to all cases arising under our fundamental law, and this power cannot be shared with foreign judges, however learned or impartial they may be. A commercial tribunal may determine whether we have violated our treaty obligations, but it cannot determine what those obligations mean under our own constitutional system.

Third, we cannot surrender the legislative power itself. Congress may bind itself to certain policies through treaty commitments, but it cannot divest itself of the authority to legislate within the sphere of its constitutional powers. Should circumstances change — should the compact prove injurious to American interests or incompatible with our domestic institutions — Congress must retain the power to withdraw from such arrangements, accepting whatever diplomatic consequences may follow.

The difficulty lies not in identifying these principles but in applying them to particular provisions of any proposed compact. A common tribunal that merely interprets commercial law and awards damages presents little danger to our sovereignty. A tribunal empowered to compel changes in our domestic legislation presents a grave danger indeed. A compact that establishes uniform standards for the quality of goods crossing borders serves legitimate commercial purposes. A compact that dictates the terms upon which American laborers may work in American factories exceeds the proper bounds of international agreement.

I would counsel the Senate to examine with particular care any provision that would establish permanent institutions with the power to issue binding regulations. The treaty power extends to agreements between nations, not to the creation of supranational governments. We fought one revolution to escape the authority of a distant legislature in which we had no voice. We should not lightly subject ourselves to another.

The test I would propose is this: Does the proposed surrender of power serve a purpose that individual states acting alone cannot accomplish, while preserving the essential capacity of the American people to govern themselves through their chosen representatives? If it meets this test, it may be within the proper scope of the treaty power. If it fails this test, no temporary commercial advantage can justify the sacrifice of republican liberty.
